title: Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Agribusiness Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think theaustralian.com.au Jared Lynch 4 May 2026 20:30 GMT 783 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem. Most of us don’t trust it. This is despite the riches it promises to deliver. Anthony Albanese, who stood with a wide grin beside Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella last month, says it could inject up to $600bn a year into the national economy by the end of the decade. Microsoft is investing $25bn on developing AI in Australia by the end of the decade. The government and CEOs say AI will turbocharge flatlining productivity, which, combined with persistent inflation, has made us all poorer, fuelling a decline in real wages. But most Australians aren’t buying it. And it’s not because we’re a nation of Luddites, according to new research from the Tech Policy Design Institute. The think tank, spun out of Australian National University last year, reveals the country’s technology stagnation is driven by a critical lack of governance. While AI has been touted as removing drudgery via the automation of menial tasks, it has prompted big job losses, with Atlassian, WiseTech Global and Afterpay owner Block sacking thousands of workers. National Australia Bank, which has created its first AI science team to help oversee a new way of working, has compared its launch to past upheavals like the “fleets” of vanishing typists and other administrative roles. Against this backdrop, the Tech Policy Design Institute’s study found only 1 per cent of Australians have complete trust in AI, while 44 per cent have no trust at all. This severe scepticism places Australia 42nd out of 47 countries in a global study on perceived trustworthiness of AI systems. Sally-Anne Williams, former CEO of tech incubator Cicada Innovations, says it comes down to poor policy. “In a world of rising disinformation and deepfakes, the need to establish and maintain that trust has never been higher. Without a trustworthy system around it, people simply won’t engage, and the value of these tools never materialises,” said Ms Williams, who is a distinguished fellow at the Tech Policy Design Institute. The report, titled Earning Trust: Unlocking AI Adoption for Australians, found that 70 per cent of Australians would be more comfortable with AI if strong government rules were already in place. It was based on a survey of 2300 Australians. “Australians aren’t anti-AI; they’re pro-accountability, and that’s a massive opportunity to grow trust from the ground up,” Ms Williams said. “We need to stop seeing regulation as a handbrake. When we get the balance right, it isn’t an enemy of innovation – it’s the infrastructure that allows it to scale and stick without impeding progress. “The mandate is undeniable. Government needs to lead, and industry needs to show up. We don’t get to sit this one out.” Assistant Minister for Science, Technology and the Digital Economy, Andrew Charlton, acknowledged the findings. “Artificial intelligence has enormous potential for Australia – but as the research outlines, we won’t unlock the benefits without adequate safeguards and trust,” he said. “Our National AI Plan is focused on exactly that: building trust and confidence, minimising harms, and making sure AI works for the Australian people." The National AI plan, released late last year, rejected a European-style stand-alone AI Act, stipulating that the technology be regulated through existing frameworks based on the advice from a new AI Safety Institute. While Australians largely rejected a “one-size-fits-all” national law like the EU’s AI Act, the study found 49 per cent preferred targeted, sector-specific laws or a hybrid model combining both. The institute’s report recommended a Coordinated AI Regulation Act to establish a broad baseline while allowing specific sector regulators to set context-calibrated rules. The study uncovered a “knowledge paradox", finding that individuals with a functional understanding of AI risks are 39 per cent more likely to support regulatory intervention than those with low knowledge. This informed consensus suggests that transparent public debate about risks will inevitably increase the mandate for regulatory action. When asked what specific issues new laws should address, respondents identified privacy and retaining control over personal information as their number one concern (scoring 75 out of 100 on a relative importance index). This was followed by protecting jobs and workers’ rights (64) and preventing the spread of AI-generated misinformation (59). “Progressing outcomes on safety is a key priority for the Albanese government as it readies to get the AI Safety Institute up and running,” Dr Charlton said. summary: Despite the potential economic benefits of artificial intelligence (AI), trust in AI among Australians is alarmingly low, with only 1% expressing complete trust. The Tech Policy Design Institute's research indicates that a lack of governance and accountability is the primary reason for this scepticism. To increase AI adoption, 70% of Australians believe strong government regulations are necessary.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
theaustralian.com.au04 May 2026 20:30 GMT783 wordsJared Lynch

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Only 1% of Australians fully trust AI, while 44% have no trust at all.
70% of Australians would feel more comfortable with AI if strong government rules were in place.
The National AI Plan aims to build trust and confidence in AI technologies.
relevance: Understanding public sentiment and regulatory needs around AI is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as they navigate the integration of AI technologies in their practices.
